entry.title: How We Built a Smart Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM Using SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

In most organizations, HR data is constantly used in non-production systems—whether for testing, development, training, or troubleshooting. During my work with SAP SuccessFactors landscapes, I’ve often seen how these environments are created quickly by copying data from Production—simply because it’s convenient and readily available.

The problem is that these environments often contain real employee data. This includes names, dates of birth, national IDs, salary details, phone numbers, and home addresses. From my experience, this is where serious privacy risks begin. Even a small oversight can lead to compliance issues under data protection regulations.

At the same time, simply replacing all values with dummy data does not work. Testing requires realistic data. Reporting hierarchies must remain intact. Validations must still pass. Payroll and downstream integrations must continue to work. I’ve personally faced scenarios where masked data looked safe—but broke workflows, reports, or approval processes.

So the real requirement is not just masking—it is smart anonymization that keeps the data functional while removing all sensitive information. This realization is what led to the idea of building a more intelligent, rule-driven, and enterprise-ready data scrambling solution.

Our Approach

To address this challenge, we built a Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP).

While designing this solution, one of my key goals was to ensure that it feels native to SAP environments—secure, scalable, and easy to integrate. Instead of simply overwriting data with meaningless dummy values, the solution intelligently anonymizes employee-related information using randomized and pattern-based substitutes.

What really mattered to me during development was realism. I wanted test and sandbox systems to behave exactly like production—so validations still pass, workflows still function, and integrations remain intact—while ensuring that no real personal data is exposed.

With this approach, organizations can confidently use non-production systems for testing, training, and demos without compromising data privacy, regulatory compliance, or system integrity.

Key Features and Benefits

1. Comprehensive Data Scrambling

  • The solution scrambles all sensitive HR fields, including employee names, dates of birth, national IDs, phone numbers, email addresses, home addresses, compensation-related information, and more.
  • What makes this approach different is that it preserves referential integrity across key SuccessFactors entities such as Employee Profile, Job Information, Employment Details, and Position data. Relationships between employees, managers, and organizational structures remain intact even after scrambling.
  • As a result, the output remains realistic and fully usable for functional testing, regression testing, and integration testing, ensuring that business processes continue to behave as expected.

2. Configurable Scrambling Rules

  • The solution allows users to define scrambling rules directly from the application at entity level.
  • It supports multiple anonymization techniques, including Randomization, Pattern-based masking, Substitution lists, Pseudonymization
  • These rules are highly flexible and can be tailored to meet specific project requirements, regional regulations, or internal compliance policies.
  • The configuration framework is also designed to support future enhancements, allowing new entities and rule types to be added easily without redesigning the solution.

3. Flexible Execution Options

The solution provides flexible execution modes to suit different project and compliance needs. Users can choose to:

  1. Run full scrambling across the entire dataset
  2. Perform partial scrambling based on Employee groups, Countries, and Selected modules

A simulation mode is also available, allowing users to preview scrambled values before applying them. This helps validate the rules and avoid unintended changes. This selective execution capability ensures that scrambling can be tailored precisely to the required scope without impacting unnecessary data.

4. Realistic and Functional Output

A key design principle of the solution is to ensure that the scrambled data remains fully functional. All transformed values are generated in valid, system-compatible formats, including:

  • Valid date formats
  • Properly structured email addresses and phone numbers
  • Preserved manager–employee hierarchy
  • Country-specific and locale-aware formatting

As a result, business processes, workflows, validations, and integrations continue to function normally—just without exposing any real personal information.

5. Download Scrambled Data

  • The solution provides a secure option to download scrambled data as exportable files for further analysis and audit purposes.
  • Users can easily export anonymized datasets along with summary logs in standard formats such as CSV and Excel.
  • These downloadable reports make it simple to perform data validation, compliance checks, and quality reviews, ensuring transparency and traceability of the scrambling process.

6. Email Notifications

Once a scrambling job is completed, the system automatically sends an email notification to the user. The notification includes:

  • Access to the scrambled output
  • Processing summary
  • Job completion status

This ensures timely communication and eliminates the need for manual follow-ups or repeated system checks. Users can conveniently access the finalized scrambled dataset directly from their email.

7. Secure SAP SuccessFactors Integration

The solution integrates securely with SAP SuccessFactors using:

  • OAuth-secured OData APIs, 
  • SAP Destination Service, and
  • SAP BTP runtime

All data extraction, scrambling, and updates are performed securely within SAP Business Technology Platform.

Architecture and Technical Stack

The Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors is deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and follows a secure, cloud-native architecture.

Users access the application through SAP Build Work Zone, which acts as the central entry point. The App Router loads the user interface from the HTML5 App Repository and handles authentication using SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) or a federated identity provider.

Once authenticated, all requests are routed securely to the backend using SAP Destination Service.The backend is built using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). It contains the complete scrambling logic and communicates with SAP SuccessFactors through trusted OData APIs.

To ensure traceability and audit readiness, all activities—such as job execution, errors, and processing details—are logged using SAP Application Logging Service.

This architecture ensures:

  • Secure data flow
  • Proper authorization
  • Controlled access
  • Full auditability

Solution Flow

  1. User launches the Data Scrambling application from SAP Build Work Zone
  2. Frontend UI (SAPUI5) is loaded via the App Router
  3. User authentication happens through SAP IAS
  4. Requests are forwarded to the CAP backend using SAP Destination Service
  5. Backend fetches data from SAP SuccessFactors via OData APIs
  6. Scrambling rules are applied to sensitive fields
  7. Logs and audit details are recorded
  8. Final scrambled output is generated and shared

Business Value

  • This solution delivers strong business value by addressing both compliance and operational challenges.
  • It protects employee privacy by ensuring that real personal data is never exposed in test, development, or training environments. This significantly reduces the risk of data leaks and regulatory violations.
  • At the same time, it keeps test environments realistic and fully functional. By preserving data formats, relationships, and system behavior, teams can continue to test workflows, reports, and integrations without disruption.
  • The solution also reduces manual effort by automating the entire anonymization process. There is no longer a need for custom scripts, ad-hoc masking, or manual cleanup activities.
  • By eliminating manual handling of sensitive data, it lowers the risk of human errors that often occur during data copying or cleansing.
  • Finally, the solution is designed to be secure and scalable, making it suitable for both small teams and large enterprise landscapes.

Conclusion

Using real HR data in non-production systems introduces serious privacy and compliance risks. At the same time, relying on unrealistic dummy data makes testing ineffective and unreliable.

This Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM bridges that gap by providing a secure, realistic, and automated way to anonymize sensitive employee information. By preserving data structures, formats, and relationships, the solution enables teams to confidently test, train, and troubleshoot without exposing real personal data.

From my own experience working with SuccessFactors integrations and test environments, I’ve seen how much time and risk this approach can save. Instead of manually cleaning data or writing one-off scripts, teams can now rely on a standardized, scalable, and auditable solution.

Ultimately, this is not just about compliance—it’s about enabling innovation safely. When teams no longer have to worry about data privacy risks, they can focus on building, improving, and delivering better solutions with confidence.

entry.title: Enabling Geofencing Mobile Clock in Clock Out- SuccessFactors Time Tracking
entry.content:
Guide to Configure Geofencing in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking (Latest Release)

Introduction

With the latest SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking enhancements, Geofencing has been introduced to improve accuracy and compliance in Clock In / Clock Out scenarios.

Geofencing ensures that employees can record their working time only when they are physically present at an approved work location, using mobile GPS validation.

This blog explains how to configure Geofencing from scratch, in simple and practical steps, especially helpful for EC Time Management consultants.

What is Geofencing in Simple Words?

          Geofencing is a virtual boundary created around a work location (office, factory, site, etc.).

If an employee is inside the boundary, they can Clock In / Clock Out

If they are outside, the system blocks the time punch

📌This works only on the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App using GPS location.

  • Business Benefits
  • Prevents incorrect or fraudulent time entries
  • Ensures employees clock time from approved locations
  • Improves audit and compliance
  • Ideal for factories, retail stores, site workers, and field staff

Prerequisites

  1. Before starting configuration, ensure the following:
  2. Employee Central Time Management is enabled
  3. Time Tracking (Clock In / Clock Out) is already working
  4. SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App is enabled
  5. Required admin and employee permissions are granted

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Check Time Tracking Configuration

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Time Management Configuration

Ensure:

Time Tracking is enabled

Clock In / Clock Out is configured

A Time Recording Profile exists

Step 2: Enable Mobile Access

Geofencing works only on mobile.

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Mobile Settings

Ensure:

SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App is enabled

Location services are allowed

Step 3: Create or Verify Location

Geofencing is linked to a Location object.

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Organization, Pay and Job Structures → Location

Create or edit a location with:

Accurate address

Country

Latitude & Longitude (auto-filled)

📌Correct address is critical for GPS accuracy.

Step 4: Create Geofence

Enable permission:

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data

Search for: Geofence

Click Create New

Fill in:

Geofence Name (example: Chennai Fence)

Status: Active

Shape Type:

Circle (recommended for beginners)

Polygon (advanced custom shape)

Use the map to draw the boundary around the workplace and save.

Step 5: Assign Geofence to Configure Clock in Clock out groups

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data → Configure Clock in Clock out groups

Save

Step 6: Assign Profiles to Employees

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data → Job Information

Ensure employees have:

Correct Location assigned

Correct Time Recording Profile

Without this mapping, geofencing will not work.

Step 7: Employee Mobile Setup

Employees must:

  • Install SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App
  • Allow Precise Location – Always Allow
  • Use Clock In / Clock Out from mobile

📌Desktop time recording does not support geofencing.

Testing Scenarios

1. Outside Geofence Location: When accessing the Clock In/Out tab in the mobile app while outside the geofenced area, the punch options were greyed out, indicating that the user is outside the allowed area for time tracking.

2. Within Geofence: As configured when user is in meters range of the location configured, Clock In/Out becomes active. The user can successfully clock in and out.

Other Scenarios:

Inside geofence : Clock In allowed ✅

Outside geofence: Clock In blocked ❌

GPS disabled: Time entry blocked ❌

Common Issues & Tips

Testing on desktop → ❌ Not supported

Incorrect location address → ❌ GPS mismatch

Mobile location permission set to “While using app” → ❌

VPN or mock GPS apps → ❌

Conclusion

Geofencing in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking is a powerful feature that ensures location-based time compliance.

When configured correctly, it significantly improves time accuracy and prevents misuse.

This feature is especially useful for:

Manufacturing units

Retail stores

On-site project teams

Field workforce

Hope my blog was useful for many consultants. Will be back with new blog. Stay Tuned!

Regards, 
Aravind R
SAP HXM Consultant

entry.title: Using SuccessFactors Onboarding for Country Transfers Without Recruiting
entry.content:
In this article, we discuss how employees who are moving to a legal entity in another country can be onboarded using SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, without SuccessFactors Recruiting.

The scenario covered here is a country transfer, not a global assignment. The employee’s employment in the source country ends, and a new employment is created in the target country.

Scenario Description

  • Current country: India
  • Target country: United States
  • Movement type: Country Transfer (not Global Assignment)
  • SuccessFactors Recruiting: Not used
  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS): Enabled
  • Onboarding module: Used for New Hire / Rehire

In this scenario, the employee will be treated as a new hire in the USA, while their employment in India is terminated.

Before we look at this process let us reconfirm the process with SuccessFactors Recruiting.

When onboarding is initiated from SuccessFactors Recruiting, the first ‘Rehire Check’ is initiated, this will trigger an employment profile selection check for the rehire co-ordinators.

They will only be able to confirm ‘Rehire on New Employment’ if the current ‘India’ record has a termination date that is before the hire date on the new ‘USA’ employment.    

Once they confirm ‘Rehire with New Employment’ an email is sent to the employee asking them to complete the onboarding process using the employment profile switcher in the top right corner as shown below.

If the employee has not applied for a role in SuccessFactors Recruiting, and SuccessFactors Recruiting is enabled, it is best practice to quickly create a requisition and add the employee to it so that the process is initiated as above.

If you do not have SuccessFactors Recruiting, then the following is a process that can be used.

The first rehire check is only initiated from SuccessFactors Recruiting, it will not trigger from ‘Add a new hire to onboarding’, this is on the roadmap but does not have a delivery date.

Step 1 – Initiate onboarding using ‘Add New Hire to Onboarding’

It is imperative that the email that is used is the personal email address and not the business email address.  The reason for this is the business email address is active in IAS.  You can use ‘business rules’ in onboarding to send out a specific welcome email for ‘Country Transfers’.

Dear XX

As we start your transfer we would like you to complete some basic information, once we have this we can use this data to allow you to finalize your transfer in SuccessFactors…..

Step 2 – Employee receives IAS email

Employee receives the IAS email to their personal email address and completes Personal Data Collection.

Step 3 – Rehire re-verification is triggered  

The second rehire verification is triggered.  The rehire co-ordinators can confirm a Rehire if there is a termination date on the India employment that is before the hire date of the USA employment.

Without termination date

With termination date

Step 4 – Legal Entity Transfer email is sent

Once the rehire is accepted the onboarding system sends the ‘legal entity transfer’ email to the business email address.  The system allows the employee to finish the onboarding process using the ‘employment profile switcher’.  Once they finish the onboarding process they land in Manage Pending Hires.

 

This process allows ‘Country Transfer’ to complete onboarding when SuccessFactors Recruiting is not enabled.

entry.title: Sample value drivers for effective Business Value Realization of your Public Cloud Solution
entry.content:
In this blog, I continue with the topic of Business Value Realization. If you are reading this blog for the first time and you have not read my value series, then I suggest you look at these two blogs first to understand the full concept of value realization using the help of SAP for Me for SAP Public Cloud Solutions: Drive more Business Value out of your SAP Public Cloud Solution Subscriptions and SAP for Me – Adoption Report – Drive More Business Value Realization.

Here, I would like to focus on a simple example of how an SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) live customer can start with a quick, comprehensive analysis to add more business outcomes using some of the value drivers SAP provides in SAP for Me, with a high number of solution capabilities for SAP Cloud ERP.

Now, keep in mind these value drivers are not the only ones you can use, but I want to recommend them to you based on my experience and the fact they will help you improve multiple areas.

So for my example, I will use an SAP Cloud ERP customer utilizing finance, sales, procurement, logistics, and manufacturing.

Now, to simplify the analysis and example, I will only focus on two value drivers for the following improvement areas to make a comprehensive analysis:

  • Reduce selling, general, and administrative expenses
  • Increase revenue
  • Decrease cost of goods sold (COGS)
  • Improve working capital
  • Improve pre-tax margin

Sample value drivers by improvement area

Now, with these value drivers, I can go back to SAP for Me and use the Customer Insight Dashboard Cloud Functional Usage under the Business Value section to look at the solution areas my business can adapt to impact these value drivers more and bring greater business outcomes to your organization.

Let’s start looking at lowering our selling, general, and administrative expenses. Notice that after adding the value drivers “Reduce account receivable management costs” and “Reduce invoice error rate,” SAP for Me displays these results.

SAP for Me – Customer Insights Dashboard – Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

Value Driver Selection - SAP for Me – Customer Insights Dashboard – Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

Selling, general, and administrative expenses - “Reduce account receivable management costs” and “Reduce invoice error rate” value drivers

The customer is using 4 solution capabilities out of 10.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in selling, general and administrative expenses.

Our customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 6 additional solution capabilities. Of course, as previously discussed in my blogs, customers can compare the unused solution capabilities with the industry peer group to be more focused on the activation. All this information can be reached via SAP for Me.

Moving now to the Increase Revenue area and the value drivers “Reduce manufacturing cycle time” and “Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment Issues,” SAP for Me displays the results.

Revenue - “Reduce manufacturing cycle time” and “Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment Issues” value drivers

The customer is using 12 solution capabilities out of 15.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in revenue

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 3 additional solution capabilities.  

Within the Decreased Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) section, you’ll notice that when value drivers like “Reduce total manufacturing costs” and “Reduce asset maintenance cost” are added, SAP for Me displays the corresponding results.

Cost of goods sold - “Reduce total manufacturing costs” and “Reduce asset maintenance cost” value drivers

The customer is using 10 solution capabilities out of 15.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in cost of goods sold

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 5 additional solution capabilities.

When you add the value drivers "Reduce sales days outstanding" and "Reduce days in inventory" to improve the Working Capital area, SAP for Me displays the resulting outcomes.

Working capital – “Reduce sales days outstanding" and "Reduce days in inventory" value drivers

The customer is using 13 solution capabilities out of 30.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in working capital

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 17 additional solution capabilities.

If you add the value drivers of “Improved on-time delivery” and “Reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs”, SAP for Me shows the following results for improving the Pre-Tax Margin area.

Pre-Tax Margin - “Improved on-time delivery” and “Reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs” value drivers

The customer is using 20 solution capabilities out of 35.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in pre-tax margin

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 15 additional solution capabilities.

Now, summing it all up, to create a comprehensive analysis on additional value realization, here is the final view using the recommended value driver by improvement area. As you can see from the graphic, the yellow area provides us with an additional opportunity for value creation using solution capabilities currently not used, which can bring new improvements and impact the value drivers.

Additional opportunity for value creation in selected improvement areas

Now, realistically speaking, implementing all these solution capabilities can be arduous, so here I recommend thinking about adding another strategy, such as filtering by benchmarking with your industry peers, to truly reduce the scope and only select the ones that are proven to be heavily used by your industry peers. If you would like to see how to do this, refer to my previous blog "Drive more Business Value out of your SAP Public Cloud Solution Subscriptions."

Now, this example can also apply to other public cloud subscriptions like SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Integrated Business Planning, etc., so feel free to play with the value drivers included in SAP for Me to find the areas of improvement you want to drive for your business.

Keep in mind that solution capabilities are expanding from traditional functional scope to also include SAP Business AI, so SAP will continue to add more in this area. As in my previous blogs, remember, this is not the only tool that can be used; SAP has other tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP WalkMe, SAP LeanIX, SAP Value Lifecycle Manager, and/or SAP Cloud ALM that can be used in tandem to help drive more business value from your SAP subscription.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations and guidance.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: Integrating External Exchange Rate API with SAP SuccessFactors for Training MDF Currency Conversions
entry.content:
Introduction :

One of my Clients has been using Training Custom MDF for nearly a year now and they always found the currency conversion the hardest , going through each requests , converting the cross -currency to their native currency. You can also ask the employees to fill in the Currency Conversion Rates but that's always not the best way out , This is a  Gap in the Process which needs to be addressed and with Intelligent System like the SAP SuccessFactors this should be easy.

Lets Break down the Configuration into Pieces :

Before we deep dive , Lets get the Scope Defined!

  1. Currency Exchange Rate MDF
  2. Exchange Rate API
  3. Integration Flow to Automate the Import Process
  4. Lookup Rule
  5. Currency Conversion 

Technical Architecture : 

Currency Exchange Rate MDF:

SAP SuccessFactors has provided a Generic Object named 'Currency Exchange Rate' which is the source of data for the Currency Conversion. The Object consists of the following fields

  1. Currency Exchange Rate Type which should always be set to DEFAULT
  2. Source Currency
  3. Target Curency
  4. Exchange Rate

\

(Screenshot of the Sample Data from Manage Data -> Currency Exchange Rate)

For details on the Currency Exchange Rate MDF , Please checkout the link below :

Setting Up the Currency Exchange Rate | SAP Help Portal

Lets get to know the most interesting part of the blog!

Nobody wants to do manual work everyday (updating Currency Exchange Rates) for each target currency. That's where things get little techincal and interesting!

I wanted to automate this process End-to-End ensuring seamless automation and what sparked is the below

Automation of the Currency Exchange Rates using API : 

There are multiple open source Exchange Rate APIs out there , the one I prefer and recommend is the  ExchangeRate-API which is reliable and Quick to Setup .Attaching the link beow

ExchangeRate-API - Free & Pro Currency Converter API

The Billing Plan is free and gives access to 1500 API calls / Month and the update is every 24 hours. If you need faster and real time currency updates, you can consider subscribing to their Paid Plans. The API endpoint is so simple and standard.

Sample End Point :

https://v6.exchangerate-api.com/v6/YOUR-API-KEY/latest/Currency

Replace the YOUR-API-KEY with your unique key displayed in the Dasboard and Currency should be replaced by your desired Three-Digit currency code.

Sample API Response:

 

 "result":"success",
 "documentation":"https://www.exchangerate-api.com/docs",
 "terms_of_use":"https://www.exchangerate-api.com/terms",
 "time_last_update_unix":1769731201,
 "time_last_update_utc":"Fri, 30 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000",
 "time_next_update_unix":1769817601,
 "time_next_update_utc":"Sat, 31 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000",
 "base_code":"USD",
 "conversion_rates":{
  "USD":1,
  "AED":3.6725,
  "AFN":65.3799,
  "ALL":80.7961,
  "AMD":379.0431,
  "ANG":1.7900,
  "AOA":919.4031,
  "ARS":1452.2500,
  "AUD":1.4186
}
}

 

Integration iFlow:

The most exciting and technical part of the blog is building the Integration Flow to automate the Currency Exchange Rate Updation Process.

Here is the Step by Step Guide to Build the iFlow:

Scheduler:

Scheduler is the start event for the integration flow. I have scheduled it to run once daily based on my preferrable timing. You can schedule it once, multiple based on your requirement.

Request Reply:

Request Reply is used to make an API Call to fetch the Currency Exchange Rate (GET).Add a Receiver and the adapter type is HTTP.

JSON to XML Converter:

JSON to XML Converter is used to convert the API response from JSON to XML format to upsert the records to SuccessFactors.

XLST Mapping:

The most crucial element of the integration Flow is the XLST Mapping which is basically to transform the converted XML from one format to another. Let me tell why this is need , the converted JSON TO XML Response looks like this

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" ?>
<result>success</result>
<documentation>https://www.exchangerate-api.com/docs</documentation>
<terms_of_use>https://www.exchangerate-api.com/terms</terms_of_use>
<time_last_update_unix>1769731201</time_last_update_unix>
<time_last_update_utc>Fri, 30 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000</time_last_update_utc>
<time_next_update_unix>1769817601</time_next_update_unix>
<time_next_update_utc>Sat, 31 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000</time_next_update_utc>
<base_code>USD</base_code>
<conversion_rates>
<USD>1</USD>
<AED>3.6725</AED>
<AFN>65.3799</AFN>
<ALL>80.7961</ALL>
<AMD>379.0431</AMD>
</conversion_rates>

 

This will not serve our purpose as all the currency exchange rates are nested inside the  Target Currencies. Our  Response should like this

 

<currency>XXX<currency>
<rate>YYY</rate>

 

The Above format is necessary for us to map the Currency  to the Target currency field and Rate to the Currency Exchange Rate field of the Currency Exchange Rate MDF in SuccessFactors.

This is where XLST Mapping comes Handy. Copy paste the below in the processing section of the XLST Section.

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"
                version="1.0">
    
    <!-- Identity template: copy everything by default -->
    <xsl:template match="@*|node()">
        <xsl:copy>
            <xsl:apply-templates select="@*|node()"/>
        </xsl:copy>
    </xsl:template>
    
    <!-- Transform conversion_rates -->
    <xsl:template match="conversion_rates">
        <rates>
            <xsl:for-each select="*">
                <rate>
                    <currency>
                        <xsl:value-of select="name()"/>
                    </currency>
                    <value>
                        <xsl:value-of select="."/>
                    </value>
                </rate>
            </xsl:for-each>
        </rates>
    </xsl:template>
    
</xsl:stylesheet>

 

This will transform the XML into our Desired Structure.

Content Modifier:

Content Modifier is used here to set two exchange properties Base Code and Last Update Date UTC.

The reason behind setting exchange properties is explained in the next section!

Iterating Splitter :

Since our API Response has multiple Currency Exchange rates , it is necessary for us to split the response to form individual XML request payload for the upsert.

The reason for setting exchange properties is because of the iterating splitter as the base code and last update UTC data are lost during the split.

Groovy Script:

Add a Groovy Script step to the iFlow to convert the Last Update UTC data to the SuccessFactors accepted ISO Format.

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.time.Instant
import java.time.ZoneOffset
import java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // Get UNIX timestamp from exchange property "lastupdate"
    def unixTime = message.getProperty("lastupdate") as long

    // Convert UNIX timestamp to ISO 8601 format
    def lastupdateIso = Instant.ofEpochSecond(unixTime)
                               .atOffset(ZoneOffset.UTC)
                               .format(DateTimeFormatter.ISO_DATE_TIME)

    // Set as new exchange property
    message.setProperty("lastupdate", lastupdateIso)

    return message
}

 

XLST Mapping:

Add an another XLST Mapping to add the base code and Last Update UTC to each of the split individual XML Payload.

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"
                version="1.0">

    <xsl:output method="xml" indent="yes"/>

    <!-- Parameters passed from CPI exchange properties -->
    <xsl:param name="basecode"/>
    <xsl:param name="lastupdate"/>

    <!-- Template for individual <rate> node -->
    <xsl:template match="rate">
        <root>
            <base_code>
                <xsl:value-of select="$basecode"/>
            </base_code>

            <time_last_update_unix>
                <xsl:value-of select="$lastupdate"/>
            </time_last_update_unix>

            <currency_code>
                <xsl:value-of select="currency"/>
            </currency_code>

            <value>
                <xsl:value-of select="value"/>
            </value>
        </root>
    </xsl:template>

</xsl:stylesheet>

 

Message Mapping:

The Crucial part of the iFlow is the message mapping where the XML payload is mapped to the Currency Exchange Rate MDF fields. I have added the mapping configuration below.Most of it is straight forward except the external code which is explained below

TARGETMAPPING
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/currencyExchangeRateTypeconst(
"DEFAULT")
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/exchangeRate/root/value
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/sourceCurrency/root/base_code
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/targetCurrency/root/currency_code
/CurrencyExchangeRate/root
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/root
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/effectiveStartDate/root/time_last_update_unix
/CurrencyExchangeRate/CurrencyExchangeRate/externalCodeconcat(
concat(
const(
"DEFAULT")
,/root/base_code,"-")
,/root/currency_code,"-")

If the External code is not mapped this way (DEFAULT-BASECURRENCY-TARGETCURRENCY) , multiple effective records won't be created and external code already exists (500 Server Error will be thrown).

Request Reply:

Add a Request Reply , add a Receiver and Setup Connection to the SuccessFactors and use the Method UPSERT.

 

Crential name is to be created in Security Material

End :

End Event to officially mark the end of the  Automation. Deploy and witness the Magic in Action!

Lookup Rule:

Now its time to assign the Lookup Rule to the Training MDF / Any MDF where Currency Conversion is required .

Replace the Source Currency with your Native Currency 

Currency Conversion:

Everything coming together in the MDF Portlet!

Conclusion :


Currency conversion is one of those areas in SuccessFactors that works really well once it’s set up correctly—but getting there can be painful if everything is done manually. In our case, maintaining exchange rates for Training MDF quickly became time-consuming and error-prone, especially when multiple currencies were involved.

By using the standard Currency Exchange Rate MDF along with a simple API-based integration, we were able to fully automate the exchange rate update process. The integration runs on a schedule, pulls the latest rates, transforms the data into the required format, and upserts it into SuccessFactors without any manual effort. Once deployed, it just works.

The real value of this solution is not just automation, but consistency. Exchange rates are always up to date, lookup rules behave as expected, and users no longer need to worry about entering or validating currency values. The same approach can also be reused for other MDFs where currency conversion is required, making it a scalable and reusable design.

Hopefully, this blog helps anyone facing similar challenges with currency conversion in SuccessFactors. If you’re dealing with manual exchange rate maintenance today, this is a small investment that can save a lot of effort in the long run.

Happy integrating! 🙂

entry.title: 06 SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation - Complex Requirements Process Design with Example- Part 2
entry.content:
This blog walks through the complex requirements design process in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation projects. The Complex Requirements Process Design blog series is structured into two parts:

 

  • Part 1: Focuses on the process design framework, supporting tools, governance model, and ownership across functional and technical teams. 

    https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/05-sap-successfactors-hcm-implementation-complex-requirements-process/ba-p/14305678                         

        

  • Part 2: Illustrates the process design approach through a detailed implementation example, highlighting design decisions, dependencies, and technical design. 

 

1. Customer Requirement:

 

 The global customer has been operating SAP S/4HANA HCM (migrated from SAP ERP HCM) for over 21 years, supporting Personnel Administration (PA), Organizational Management (OM), and Payroll for U.S. and Canadian employees. The HCM landscape has evolved over time, and complex customizations have been built to support diverse requirements.  Current system has a complex process to handle the Temporary Assignment (TA) to meet complex North America–specific business and compliance requirements.

As part of the SAP SuccessFactors implementation, PA and OM will transition to Employee Central (EC), integrated with SuccessFactors Talent modules, while Payroll will remain on SAP S/4HANA via EC–Payroll integration. This hybrid deployment introduces a critical design requirement: the existing Temporary Assignment process must be re-engineered in SuccessFactors Employee Central without disrupting S4HANA payroll, Connected, or downstream integrations.

Temporary Assignment refers to a situation where an employee works in a role outside their regular scope for a defined period (minimum of one day), while remaining under their existing employment contract.

 

 2. Requirement analysis :  

 

Understanding the requirements is the first and most critical phase of the design process. This phase focused on gaining a deep understanding of the customer’s existing Temporary Assignment (TA) framework, which—while highly customized—has proven to be stable, scalable, and effective.

To ensure the SuccessFactors design fully supports the current-state behavior, detailed discussions were conducted with process owners, functional leads, and technical teams to analyze how the TA process is currently configured and executed in SAP S/4HANA HCM. The following activities were completed as part of this phase:

  1. End-to-end walkthrough of the current Temporary Assignment process led by the customer, covering both functional and payroll-relevant steps
  2. Collection and review of existing current-state documentation, including process flows, configuration details, and custom logic
  3. Detailed analysis of S/4HANA HCM configuration, identifying standard versus custom components supporting the TA process
  4. Assessment of current challenges and pain points, including operational constraints and system limitations
  5. Identification of dependency items, such as infotypes, workflows, integrations, reporting, and downstream systems
  6. Impact analysis, evaluating how moving PA and OM to Employee Central affects the S4HCM and Time/Benefits Applications process and its payroll touchpoints.

These structured requirements analysis established a baseline for solution design, ensuring that the SuccessFactors implementation preserves existing business outcomes while minimizing risk to payroll and dependent processes.

 

3. Design Considerations and Dependencies: 

 

Following the requirements analysis, a high-level process flow was created to illustrate the key systems, integration points, and business processes involved in the Temporary Assignment requirement. Given the multiple interdependencies across Employee Central, SAP S/4HANA HCM, and downstream processes, it was critical to visualize the end-to-end flow before progressing to detailed solution design.

Each identified dependency was then analyzed individually, with a focus on functional behavior, integration impact, and payroll sensitivity. For every dependency, a detailed design approach was defined to ensure that changes introduced in SuccessFactors Employee Central do not adversely affect existing processes or payroll outcomes.

In collaboration with the customer, a comprehensive requirements and design document was developed covering all functional, technical, and integration aspects of the Temporary Assignment process. This artifact served as a single source of truth, providing full visibility into process dependencies, data ownership, system responsibilities, and impact areas, and enabled informed design decisions for the target-state solution.

 

4. Employee Central and RCM Design :

 

Designing the SAP SuccessFactors solution is a critical aspect of this implementation, as the Temporary Assignment process spans multiple systems and initiation points. The target-state design had to accommodate existing system behavior while ensuring data consistency, process continuity, and payroll integrity. The following key design considerations guided the SuccessFactors solution:

Key Requirement considerations for SF functional design:

 

  • Temporary Assignment initiation from an existing external application
    Temporary Assignments are primarily initiated through an existing external application (TAIA) that includes its own approval processes and workflows. There is no plan to replace this system; therefore, the future-state design ensures that this application continues to function as the system of initiation, with seamless integration into SuccessFactors Employee Central.
  • Temporary Assignment initiation from SuccessFactors Recruiting (Internal Hire scenarios)
    In select scenarios, Temporary Assignments are initiated via SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM) for internal hire rotations. The design accounts for these scenarios by aligning RCM.
  • Temporary Assignment extensions and terminations across systems
    For most scenarios, Temporary Assignment extensions and terminations are managed directly in Employee Central. However, the design also supports cases where these actions are initiated from the external application, requiring bidirectional integration and robust event handling to maintain data synchronization.
  • Identifier generation for existing Temporary Assignment records
    In the SAP S/4HANA system, personnel numbers were not generated for Temporary Assignments. As part of the migration to Employee Central, a strategy was defined to generate unique User IDs and/or Assignment IDs for existing Temporary Assignment Employment records. Post go-live, Employee Central becomes the system of record and will automatically generate these identifiers for all new Temporary Assignments and generate the IDs.

Functional Design to Demo:

 

  • Functional Requirements & Mapping: Created a detailed decision matrix and use-case document to capture and map all functional requirements.
  • EC Design Perspective: From an Employee Central (EC) design point of view, the solution is straightforward as standard EC Temporary Assignment (Higher Duty) functionality supports it.
  • Configuration & Demonstration: Enabled Temporary assignment in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central (SF EC).Demonstrated the configured solution to the customer, showing the end-to-end user experience of how it works in different scenarios.
  • RCM Design: Designed a detailed process for RCM and RCM – EC data sync.

Introduction of MDF Object for  Hold Temporary Assignment Details :

 We have introduced an MDF object to hold Temporary Assignment details. The rationale for creating this object is outlined below:

  • Simplify Interface Complexity
    • Current interfaces contain extremely complex logic.
    • Using the MDF object as a single source of truth reduces complexity and standardizes data handling.
    • External systems no longer need to combine data from multiple employment portlets.
  • Capture Deletions Efficiently
    • Data Hub and other external systems need to be notified when a Temporary Assignment is deleted.
    • Without the MDF object, writing logic to extract deleted records across multiple API calls becomes complex.

 

5. Temporary Assignment Data Migration Approach: 

 

The primary challenge is migrating Temporary Assignment data from S/4HANA and mapping it correctly to the Employee Central (EC) data model.

 

  • Data Migration Scope: Top-of-stack data. Any employee with an active Temporary Assignment and any future-dated Temporary Assignment records were migrated.
  • Data Extraction Logic: We used InfoPorter for S4HANA Data Migration, and a separate template group was created that includes all Temporary Assignment portlets to standardize extraction and transformation rules.
  • Data Migration Dependencies: Active Employment data must be successfully migrated before migrating Temporary Assignment data.
  • Number Range Assignment: Added the 90-series to Personnel Number during migration for User ID and Assignment ID. This ensures migrated records are easily identifiable.   
  •    Temporary Assignment Portlets Sequence: 

6. Technical Integrations Design:

 

 The integrations have the greatest impact, and we need to support existing data contracts across all systems, ensuring no impact on payroll.

 

 7. End-to-End High-Level Design:

 

Below is the overall design implemented, along with a few things that evolved during implementation.

8. Design contributions, implementation, and challenges: 

 

  • Contributions from Solution Architects, Functional Leads, Technical Leads, Technical Architects, Data Migration Architects, and the customer team were critical.

 

  • Certain design aspects were adjusted during implementation. Initially, we planned to sync Temp Assignment Employment Portlet details to MDF through Business/Integration Center. However, the deletion requirement necessitated using a different API, so we implemented it via SAP IS.

 

  • During implementation, we identified that the planned end date could not be updated through the API. We collaborated with the SAP product team to enhance this functionality.

 

9. Conclusion: 

 

  • When designing solutions for any requirement—big or small—the critical factors are that it must not disrupt payroll and that there is minimal or no manual work.
  •  Never underestimate complexity based on high-level requirements. A detailed analysis is essential to capture all requirements and dependencies.
  • Having skilled resources and an open mind to consider different design ideas is key to success.
  • Document the reasoning behind each design decision recommendation. This prevents repeated explanations and saves the team time in the long run.

 

PS: I have generalized the requirement a bit due to the sensitivity of some requirements, and don’t want to reveal customer-specific data. If you have any specific questions or want to learn more, please DM me.

entry.title: Reporting Actual Working Time When Breaks Are Recorded in Different Ways
entry.content:
The approach is not perfect (≈98% accurate) and works only if cancelled timesheets are excluded (see my last post on timesheet integration for details).  Before we start with the report story, we first need to understand what kind of break types are possible in time tracking. 

 Breaks can appear in timesheets in three ways:

  • Separate break entry (non-overlapping)
    A break is recorded with the time type “Break” between two working-time entries.
  • Separate break entry (overlapping)
    A break is recorded with the time type “Break” but overlaps with a working-time entry.
  • Implicit break
    No break is recorded explicitly; instead, there is a gap between two working-time entries.

Now that we know all the ways breaks can be recorded, lets create the report. For better understanding I identified four different steps to be taken:

  1. Collect all relevant timesheet entries.
  2. Create calculated columns to:
    • Sum working time
    • Sum break time
    • Count working-time entries
  3. Build a report table.
  4. Calculate actual working time based on the number of working-time entries per day.

 

1.     Collect all relevant timesheet entries.

Start from the Employee Timesheet domain and add Employee Timesheet Entry as well as Employee Time and Time Type. Join additional domains as needed (via User or Job Info—either is fine). 

In terms of fields make sure to select all the relevant date fields and the number of hours from the Employee Time entity as well as the external code from the time type entity. Everything else can be selected as you need it.

Now add a filter to the selected data base and filter the timesheet approval status to:

  • Approved
  • To Be Approved
  • To Be Submitted

Exclude cancelled entries .We don't want the cancelled entries in the report as they will generate duplicates. If you are not sure why, I have written a time tracking integration blogpost in which i explain how the timesheet audit is set up.

 

2.     Required Measures

Now for the calculations. Create the following calculated measures (all aggregated with SUM)

 

Brutto Working Time (“Bruttoarbeitszeit”)
Break ("Pause")
Number of Timesheet Entries (“Anzahl Timesheeteinträge”)
Sum of working-time hours onlySum of break hours only

Counts how many working-time entries exist per day. This measure is later aggregated in the report story.

Assumption: If there is only one working-time entry, the break is likely recorded separately using a break time type.

If (time type code="working time",Number of hours,0)

If (time type code="break",Number of hours,0)

 If( time type code= "working time",1,0)

 

 3.    Create the cross-tab table report

We have everything we need in order to create a cross-tab table and report on the actual working time. Select all of the columns you need. You can select the calculated columns to be displayed if you want to, but this is not mandatory.

  

 

4.    Calculate actual working time  - where the magic happens

 Create a calculated story measure that adjusts working time based on how breaks are recorded:

IF( Number of timesheetentries=1, Brutto working time - Break,Brutto working time)

 Logic: If there is only one working-time entry, subtract the explicitly recorded break. Else If there are multiple working-time entries, I assume breaks are already implicit.

 

 

 And voilà -  add the calculation as a measure to your table report 🙂

Now you can correctly report on only working time regardless of the break type used.

Conclusion

When you link the break deduction calculation to the amount of timesheet entries you can almost accurately report on only working time regardless of the break types used. For me, this approach only worked using a report story even though I appreciate a good old canvas just as much.

entry.title: From Manual to Automated: Managing Day-in-Lieu Credits in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Introduction :

One of my Clients had a pain point where they were manually adding the Day-in-lieu credits which caused operational inefficiency and requested for an automation which would avert the need for manual intervention. Then came the Integration center and MDF Object to the rescue!

Tech Behind the Automation :

Compensation Leave Portlet :

The Compensation Leave portlet is the portal / space to request for Day-in-Lieu Credits for the Overtime Worked on Weekends / Public Holidays .The Compensation Leave Portlet captures the Basic Details of the employee such as Employee ID , Employee Name , Start Date , End Date , Position , Department of the Employee. The Reason field is added to notify approvers the necessity for the work to be carried out on rest days. The Number of days field is automatically calculated from the Start and End Date. An attachment field to add any supporting Documents.

Account Created field of Type String should be created as it is the most crucial field in the whole of this automation.

Time Type and Time Account Type Creation :

Time Type Creation :

Create a New Time Type named "Compensatory Leave " with the below mentioned configuration.

The reason behind Balance Calculation Setting is set to "Consider Bookings until Calculation Date" is because according to my Client requirement , Day-in-lieu credits are only valid for 1 month from the Approval Date and I don't want to show any leaves credits in the future (if multiple requests are present) in the Request Time Off UI.

Time Account Type : 

Create a Time Account Type with the below specified configurations.We are creating an Adhoc Account as that is the most preferred Time Account Type for Day-in-Lieu Accounts.

Assign the created Time Account Type under the Time Account Posting Rules of the Time Type. Make sure the balance calculation setting is set the same in both time type and time account type.

Integration Center :

The Engine behind the Automation , the Integration. We have the pre-requisites set already ,

  1.  Compensation Leave Portlet
  2. Compensation Time Type
  3. Compensation Time Account Type

Integration Mappings :

Source - Compensatory Leave Portlet

Destination - Time Account , Compensatory Leave Portlet

Setting the Account Validity to One month from the Date 

The reason behind also including the Compensatory Leave Portlet in the Destination is we are writing back the value to YES in the Account Created field of the Compensatory Leave Portlet.

Time Account External Code - Effective Date of the Portlet, the reason behing this to make sure any future edits doesn't result in duplication and are updated in the same time account.

Time Account Validity from and until is set to Last Modified Date

Bookable Period From - Last Modified Date

Bookable Period Until - Last Modified Date + 1 Month

The Integration Specification is attached in the Blog for easy Import

Schedule :

You can schedule the integration based on the business requirements. I prefer and advice scheduling Two integrations (One with Daily Frequency) and Other with Multiple Executions per Day with Time Based Filter (Based on Delta Changes after Last Run Time).

Both of the Integrations should be filter by the field (Account Created = null) so it only picks the records for which accounts have not been created. The Account with Daily frequency is scheduled because it will pick any missing records missed by the other integration running multiple times a day (Account Created field filter is mandatory).

Drive link of the Integration Definition File :

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1MrNS0byqRylDm8Aqr4G7LMDatkDaTQX8

Alert Rule :

To make the optimum use of SuccessFactors feature and to make things interesting , you can set a post save rule to inform employee in email and to-do about the credit of Day-in-Lieu.

Alert Message :

I have used the documentation group in the Alert Message

Document Generation Template:

Template Mapping :

Conclusion :

This automation successfully eliminates the manual effort involved in creating Day-in-Lieu credits and replaces it with a scalable, reliable, and audit-friendly solution. By leveraging the Compensation Leave MDF Object, Time Type, Time Account Type, and Integration Center, the entire lifecycle—from request approval to time account creation—is handled seamlessly without any human intervention.

The use of the Account Created flag ensures idempotency and prevents duplicate account creation, while the carefully aligned balance calculation settings between the Time Type and Time Account Type guarantee accurate visibility in the Request Time Off UI. Setting a one-month validity window from the approval date ensures strict adherence to the client’s business policy and avoids future-dated balance confusion.

Additionally, the dual-integration scheduling strategy (multiple daily runs with delta changes plus a daily fallback job) adds an extra layer of reliability, ensuring no approved requests are ever missed. This design not only improves operational efficiency but also enhances employee experience by providing timely and accurate compensatory leave credits.

Overall, this solution demonstrates how Integration Center, when combined with well-designed MDF objects and time management configurations, can solve real-world business pain points with minimal maintenance and maximum impact.

entry.title: Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignments
entry.content:
Introduction

You can use the integration of SAP’s Public Sector Management (PSM), Funds and Grants Management Solution with SAP SuccessFactors to use public sector financing objects such as funds, grants, functional area, funds center, and the budget period within SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central.

This integration helps you to allocate multiple costs and distribute them for employees and positions in various business processes such as standard employment, global assignment, and concurrent employment. Additionally, the integration also supports real-time derivation and validation of assignments from SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise financials to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central out of the box. Thus, if you have an SAP S/4HANA Cloud or SAP S/4HANA On Premise solution, you can use this integration along with real-time derivation and validation of assignments to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central out of the box.

When you allocate multiple cost assignments and distribute them for employees and positions in different business processes, you have the option of providing the percentage values for the additional cost assignments for the employees and positions.

 

Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignment

SAP Business Technology Platform Master Data Integration allows you to integrate Public Sector Master Data between SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central. If you use this integration to replicate Workforce Master Data, then decimal values provided for the percentages for the additional cost assignments are rounded off. In this case, the rounding off of these percentage values may pose a problem for your implementation of cost assignment and distribution. Therefore, SAP recommends that you do not provide decimal values for the percentages for the additional cost assignments.

However, if you do not use this integration, you might want to have the flexibility to provide decimal values for the percentages for the additional cost assignments.

To allow you the option to choose whether decimal values must be used for the percentages for the additional cost assignments, the Admin Center > Configure Public Sector Management Cost Object Assignments UI now provides an additional field under the Additional Cost Assignment checks section, called Allow Decimal Values for Percentage during Cost Assignments.

By default, this dropdown is set to “No”, so only whole numbers are allowed for the cost assignment percentages. However, if you change this dropdown value to “Yes”, you can provide decimal values for the percentages in the cost assignments.

Note: If an error (titled “Unknown property”) occurs while saving the Configure Public Sector Management Cost Object Assignments configuration, you can manually trigger the synchronization of the configuration changes. To do so, navigate to Admin Center > OData API Metadata Refresh and Export, and click the Refresh button under the Refresh Metadata section.

To summarize, you now have the option and flexibility to specify whether decimal values are allowed while specifying the percentages for the additional cost assignments in SAP’s Public Sector Management (PSM), Funds and Grants Management Solution.

entry.title: A Step-by-Step Guide to Integrating SAP SuccessFactors with Power BI through Direct OData Feed
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Why Direct OData Feed? (Comparison of Options)
  • Key Technical Challenges Solved
  • 1. Server-Side Pagination
  • 2. Performance Optimization
  • 3. Row-Level Security (RLS)
  • Implementation Guide
  • Prerequisites
  • Step 1: Create a Blank Query
  • Step 2: The Logic (M Code)
  • Step 3: Publish and Automate
  • Troubleshooting Common Issues
  • Conclusion

Introduction

In the modern enterprise, HR data is no longer just about records—it's about strategic insights. While SAP SuccessFactors is a powerhouse for Human Capital Management, organizations often struggle to visualize this data alongside financial and operational metrics. Many customers turn to Microsoft Power BI for its rich visualization capabilities and ecosystem integration. However, a native connector for SuccessFactors does exist in PowerBI. As a SuccessFactors consultant, I've seen many struggle with this integration due to limited technical resources or budget constraints. This guide provides a lightweight, cost-effective, and powerful solution using the Direct OData Feed method with empowerment of AI. By leveraging this approach, you can build modern, real-time dashboards without investing in expensive middleware or extensive data engineering resources.

Why Direct OData Feed? (Comparison of Options)

There isn't a "one-size-fits-all" solution. The best integration strategy depends on your organization's specific needs, budget, and technical maturity.

   

Integration Option
 Basic IntroductionComplexity Data LatencyBest Use caseLicense RequirementRequired Implementation resoureCost
Direct OData Feed (This Guide)A standardized protocol (OData v2/v4) based on HTTP and REST for consuming SuccessFactors data directly via native Power BI connectors(Advance Query) or Web Service APIs.Low to Medium; requires handling server-side pagination (1,000-row limit) via recursive M-code functions, URI composition, and query optimizations ($filter, $expand). SuccessFactors could complete the task with the power of AIReal-time or Scheduled Refresh; provides methods for CRUD operations directly against the application layer.Small to moderate datasets, simple reports, or scenarios requiring real-time access to logical application objects.No additional license; requires secure authentication (OAuth 2.0/OIDC).Technical expertise in RESTful APIs, Power Query (M) skills for pagination logic, and SF Administrator permissions for API Center and RBP configuration.Low
SAP DatasphereSAP's native cloud data warehouse solution within SAP BTP that creates a unified semantic layer over SuccessFactors data using federation or replication.Moderate to High; involves configuration of cloud-to-cloud connections, semantic modeling, and Graphical Views within the SAP environment.Near real-time via remote tables (federated) or scheduled replication.Organizations requiring cross-SAP module analysis, unified governance, and a centralized semantic layer for SAP and non-SAP data.SAP BTP Credits / Datasphere subscription; Power BI connector for SAP.SAP modeling expertise, Cloud Connector configuration, and Private endpoints for secure access.High
Azure Data FactoryA PaaS-based ETL/ELT service that uses native connectors or the SAP CDC (Change Data Capture) framework to ingest data into a data lake for transformation.High; involves designing multi-stage pipelines (Bronze-Silver-Gold), setting up Self-Hosted Integration Runtime (SHIR), and linked services.Near real-time or low-latency batch using Change Data Capture (CDC) via ODP framework.Large-scale enterprise data warehousing, datasets with millions of records, and complex cross-system data merging.Azure Consumption (pay-as-you-go); SAP .NET Connector (SAP NCo 3.0); may require a trusted third-party IdP for SAML assertions.Data Engineering skills, Windows VM for SHIR, and SAP Basis/Security team for ODP/RFC and Identity Provider (IdP) setup.High
3rd Party Connectors (e.g., CData)A specialized third-party Power BI Connector/Driver featuring a built-in SQL transformation layer that translates SQL queries into real-time OData calls.Low to Medium; provides a plug-and-play setup for OAuth/SAML and automated handling of API complexities like pagination.Live (DirectQuery) or scheduled batch refresh.Rapid deployment of reports without custom coding; real-time dashboards; simplified cloud-to-cloud integration via Connect AI.Third-party CData license (Connect AI or Driver); SuccessFactors API Key/Client Secret.Power BI Desktop; CData Gateway (for Service refresh); SAP Admin for OAuth registration.High

Why we chose Direct OData Feed: It allows for a rapid deployment, requires zero additional licensing, and offers full control over the data query via Power Query (M) language.

Key Technical Challenges Solved

Before we start coding, it’s crucial to understand why this isn't just a simple "Connect" button.

1. Server-Side Pagination

SuccessFactors protects its cloud infrastructure by limiting API responses to 1,000 records per page. If you simply request data, you'll only get the first 1,000 rows. Our solution uses a recursive M-code function to automatically "loop" through all pages until every record is fetched.

2. Performance Optimization

SuccessFactors tables (Entities) are wide. Fetching select * is a recipe for slow reports. We use the $select OData parameter to request only the fields we need, drastically reducing load times.

3. Row-Level Security (RLS)

HR data is sensitive. We can leverage Power BI's USERPRINCIPALNAME() function to match the logged-in viewer with their data, ensuring managers only see their direct reports.

Implementation Guide

Prerequisites

Before proceeding, ensure you have:

  1. SuccessFactors API Key & Client Secret: Recommended to use SAP Blog IAS OIDC Token Authentication as Basic Auth is being deprecated.
  2. API Permissions: The integration user must have permission to query the necessary OData entities (e.g., EmpJob, User).

Step 1: Create a Blank Query

  1. Open Power BI Desktop.
  2. Go to Get Data > Blank Query.
  3. In the Power Query Editor, click Advanced Editor on the Home ribbon.

Step 2: The Logic (M Code)

Paste the following code into the Advanced Editor. This script handles OAuth 2.0 authentication, Pagination, and Data Flattening automatically.

Note: Replace the placeholders (ClientID, CompanyID, PrivateKey) with your actual credentials through "Manager Parameters"

The AI Game-Changer: This step was once the biggest technical hurdle, but AI has completely shifted the paradigm. You no longer need to be an expert in Power Query (M) to succeed. Simply feed the sample code below into an AI tool, specify your SuccessFactors API endpoints and the fields you need, and let the AI generate the code for you. If errors arise, just paste them back into the chat—AI will troubleshoot and refine the script until it runs perfectly.

let
    // --- 1. CONFIGURATION ---
    SF_BaseURL = "https://apiKey.sapsf.cn", // Update to your data center URL
    SF_ClientID = "YOUR_CLIENT_ID",
    SF_CompanyID = "YOUR_COMPANY_ID",
    SF_UserID = "ADMIN_USER",
    SF_PrivateKey = "YOUR_PRIVATE_KEY_ONE_LINE", // Ensure no line breaks

    // --- 2. AUTHENTICATION (OAuth 2.0) ---
    // Step A: Request Assertion
    AssertionResponse = Text.FromBinary(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
        RelativePath = "/oauth/idp",
        Query = [#"$format" = "json"],
        Content = Text.ToBinary(
            "client_id=" & SF_ClientID & 
            "&user_id=" & SF_UserID & 
            "&token_url=" & SF_BaseURL & "/oauth/token" & 
            "&private_key=" & SF_PrivateKey
        ),
        Headers = [#"Content-Type"="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]
    ])),
    AssertionID = AssertionResponse,

    // Step B: Exchange Assertion for Access Token
    TokenResponse = Json.Document(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
        RelativePath = "/oauth/token",
        Content = Text.ToBinary(
            "company_id=" & SF_CompanyID & 
            "&client_id=" & SF_ClientID & 
            "&grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:saml2-bearer" & 
            "&user_id=" & SF_UserID & 
            "&assertion=" & AssertionID
        ),
        Headers = [#"Content-Type"="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]
    ])),
    AccessToken = TokenResponse[access_token],
    AuthHeader = "Bearer " & AccessToken,

    // --- 3. RECURSIVE DATA FETCHING FUNCTION ---
    GetSFData = (UrlPath as text) =>
        let
            Source = Json.Document(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
                RelativePath = UrlPath,
                Headers = [Authorization = AuthHeader]
            ])),
            Data = Source[d][results],
            // Check if there is a next page
            NextFullUrl = try Source[d][#"__next"] otherwise null,
            // Extract relative path from absolute URL
            NextPath = if NextFullUrl <> null then Text.Replace(NextFullUrl, SF_BaseURL, "") else null,
            // Recursive call if NextPath exists
            Result = if NextPath <> null then List.Combine({Data, @GetSFData(NextPath)}) else Data
        in
            Result,

    // --- 4. EXECUTION ---
    // Define your OData query here with $select and $filter for performance
    InitialPath = "/odata/v2/EmpJob?$format=json&$filter=emplStatus eq 'A'&$select=userId,businessUnit,jobTitle,jobCode,position,department,managerId,emplStatus&$expand=emplStatusNav&$top=1000",
    
    RawDataList = GetSFData(InitialPath),

    // --- 5. TRANSFORMATION ---
    #"Converted to Table" = Table.FromList(RawDataList, Splitter.SplitByNothing(), null, null, ExtraValues.Error),
    #"Expanded Columns" = Table.ExpandRecordColumn(#"Converted to Table", "Column1", 
        {"userId", "businessUnit", "jobTitle", "jobCode", "position", "department"}, 
        {"UserId", "Business Unit", "Job Title", "Job Code", "Position", "Department"})
in
    #"Expanded Columns"

Step 3: Publish and Automate

  1. Close & Apply to load the data into Power BI.
  2. Build your visualizations (Charts, Tables, KPIs).
  3. Publish to the Power BI Service.
  4. Schedule Refresh:
    • Go to the dataset settings in Power BI Service.
    • Set up a daily refresh schedule (e.g., 6:00 AM) to ensure dashboards always show the latest HR data. Note: The exction are optional because it is usually done by the Power BI Admin. SucccessFactors are mainly responsible for the data extraction and loading into the Power BI Service.

Troubleshooting Common Issues

The "Authorization Header" Paradox

  • Symptom: Power BI throws an error about the Authorization header being disallowed.
  • Cause: Power BI tries to handle authentication natively.
  • Fix: When prompted for credentials for the web source, select Anonymous. The M-code handles the actual authentication via the header manually.And Set the Privacy Level to "Organizational" for all data sources involved.

Data Truncation

  • Symptom: You only see 1,000 rows.
  • Fix: Ensure you are using the recursive function (GetSFData) shown above, not just a simple Web.Contents call.

Conclusion

appreciate all contributors online who write the relevant material about the integration and thank to this AI era. The emergence of AI tools has fundamentally streamlined the integration process. Tasks that previously required weeks of specialized learning in Power Query (M) can now be completed in a fraction of the time. By leveraging resources like Google NotebookLLM for rapid research and Gemini for code generation and debugging, I can shift their focus from writing syntax to orchestrating solutions. This evolution empowers me to deliver complex integrations efficiently, regardless of their initial technical depth in a specific tool.

Finally, I am sharing my NotebookLM notebook on SuccessFactors-PowerBI integration, providing a deep dive into the technical details and research behind this solution. Google NotebookLLM SF-PowerBI Integration

entry.title: Understanding the Onboarding USA compliance forms
entry.content:
SuccessFactors Onboarding solution delivers two standard compliance forms

  • Voluntary Self-Identification of Veteran Status
  • Voluntary Self-Identification of Disability

Both of these forms appear under the compliance section and do not need to be completed before a hire will land in Manage Pending Hires. If you enable these forms, they are on for all hires.

Whilst these are delivered as standard there are some important configuration considerations that affect most customers.  The first is how this data is stored in onboarding and employee central.  Essentially the data from these forms are not stored directly to the employee record.  It is reportable but only with an API call.

The second is that the standard employee central fields do not correspond to the fields on the standard forms particularly the veteran status form.  In this article we will look at the options available.

Voluntary Self-Identification of Disability

The first question to ask customers is do you need the employee to see the standard form or just to collect the data for reporting?  The ability to clearly and easily report on the data is critical for some customer.  Some customer state they need to present the text on the form as well. 

Step 1 Aligning the field in Recruitment & Onboarding.

It is essential that the picklist values in recruitment and onboarding share the same picklist and that these match the values needed for reporting.  Whether you choose to map the value from recruitment to onboarding is a decision for the customer. 

SAP delivers a standard picklist for the Disability values the picklist is called ‘SelfIdentificationDisabilityStatusUSA’ and whilst the labels do not align 100% with those on the form they can be easily adjusted.  Many consultants forget that this standard picklist is available.  The advantage of using the standard is that if the values change SAP will automatically update them,

This picklist is assigned to SAP standard delivered field below.

If recruitment uses the picklist ‘SelfIdentificationDisabilityStatusUSA’ then the data can flow providing the mapping is set up in recruit to hire mappings.

As standard this is how the field would look,

The label can be adjusted as follow

However, on the form there is a lot of other information, and some customers want to expose this. 

What you can’t do is map the data from the global field into the standard compliance form.   This means you have 2 options.

Option 1

  • Enable the standard fields in onboarding and employee central
  • Enable the standard form
  • Get the hire to select the correct data in PDC and then again on the compliance form. 

With this approach the user does have to select the same data twice and they could select different values.  However, they do see the standard form.

Option 2

  • Enable the standard fields in onboarding and employee central.
  • Do not enable the standard form.
  • Get the hire to select the correct data in PDC and map this data to a custom form that they sign in the e-signature step.
  • If you do not want to sign the form and so not have any other forms to sign you can do the following.  You enable the signature step in the process variant, assign the form with no signature, and this will generate an unsigned from in the onboarding dashboard.  thanks to Lisa George for this suggestion

This means the user only has to select the data in PDC and it maps to the form.  However, it means the hire must sign the form during the ‘e-signature step’ before they land in MPH.  As this is a custom form you can also only show the form for specific hires.  This can be useful for companies who may want to only show the forms that are federal contractors.  This is useful article on how you can turn custom forms on and off based on your organizational structure.

Voluntary Self-Identification of Veteran Status

The challenge with the veteran status is that the standard values delivered by SAP do confuse customers.  Many customers tend to have veteran fields that just have ‘yes/no/ answers as show below. These do not correspond to the options on the standard form.

There is a standard field and picklist delivered by SAP as highlighted below. This is a relatively new field and lots of customers still use the other fields.  If you do not want to alter the fields currently used in employee central you can enable the ‘Disclosed Veteran Category’ field just in onboarding using the onboarding person type.

In terms of options for the veteran status those listen above for disability are still relevant until the data entered in global info is mapped to the standard compliance form.

entry.title: How to Restrict Access to Employee Data Based on Responsibility Period? - SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Time‑Restricted Access to Employee Records: Why “When” and “Where” Matter! 

Rina just became the manager of Dev, who moved to her team on April 1, 2025. On her first day, Rina tries to open Dev’s entire work history to understand his past assignments back to 2019. She’s surprised: she can only see records from April 1, 2025 onward—nothing before. Meanwhile, Amol in Payroll can see Dev’s UK records but not his earlier US ones. Why the difference? Because their access is now restricted by time of responsibility and legal entity responsibility, not just by a broad role like “Manager” or “Payroll Admin.”

This blog explains, in simple terms, how period‑restricted access works in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central for managers, job relationship roles, and permission‑group (admin) roles—and why it’s a meaningful shift from traditional role‑based access that exposed all historical time slices.

What changed—and why it matters

Historically, role‑based permissioning in many HR systems allowed anyone with a role (e.g., Manager or HR Admin) to view an employee’s entire history of effective‑dated records (job info, personal info, compensation, etc.). That created two problems:

  1. Over‑exposure of history: New managers could see records from periods when they had no responsibility.
  2. Complex compliance scenarios: Admins responsible for specific legal entities sometimes saw records for employees outside those entities.

Period‑restricted access fixes this by overlaying time (and, for admins, legal entity responsibility) on top of role‑based permissions. Result: people see only the records that fall within their responsibility period, and admins see only records for in‑scope legal entities within the dates they were responsible.

Supported HRIS entities include effective‑dated data like Job Information, Job Relationships, Personal Information, Dependent Information, Address Information, and Compensation Information on the latest People Profile (view, edit, and History UI) and EC Quick Actions.
 

Key concepts at a glance

  • Responsibility period (time): The start and end dates during which a manager (or a job‑relationship role like matrix manager, HR manager, or custom manager) is formally associated to the employee. Access is limited to only those dates across the employee’s effective‑dated records.
  • Permission‑group admins (scope): For roles granted via permission groups, access is further limited to the legal entities selected as “responsible” for that role. Within those entities, time rules still apply—you see only the time slices that fall within your responsibility period.
  • Direct associations only: Period‑restricted access covers direct managers and supported job‑relationship types (e.g., HR manager, matrix manager, second/additional/custom manager). It doesn’t auto‑roll up to higher or lower org levels.
